DS 6373 Project

Arman Azhand

2025-12-07

Data

This dataset provides daily weather observations from Heathrow, United Kingdom, spanning the period from 1979 to 2023. The data is sourced from the European Climate Assessment & Dataset (ECA&D) and includes multiple weather parameters such as temperature, precipitation, sunshine, and more. The CSV file was obtained from the Kaggle London Weather Data From 1979 to 2023 dataset.

daily_data = read.csv("./project_data/london_weather_data_1979_to_2023.csv")
daily_data$DATE = as.Date(as.character(daily_data$DATE), format = "%Y%m%d")
daily_data$Avg_Temp = daily_data$TG
daily_data$Precipitation = daily_data$RR
daily_data$Humidity = daily_data$HU
daily_data$Sea_Level_Pressure = daily_data$PP

clean_data = daily_data %>% dplyr::select(DATE, Avg_Temp, Precipitation, Humidity, Sea_Level_Pressure) %>% dplyr::filter(DATE >= as.Date("2022-01-01"))
summary(clean_data)
##       DATE               Avg_Temp      Precipitation       Humidity   
##  Min.   :2022-01-01   Min.   :-21.00   Min.   :  0.00   Min.   :33.0  
##  1st Qu.:2022-07-02   1st Qu.: 85.25   1st Qu.:  0.00   1st Qu.:67.0  
##  Median :2022-12-31   Median :126.00   Median :  0.00   Median :76.0  
##  Mean   :2022-12-31   Mean   :127.98   Mean   : 18.35   Mean   :75.2  
##  3rd Qu.:2023-07-01   3rd Qu.:173.75   3rd Qu.: 18.00   3rd Qu.:84.0  
##  Max.   :2023-12-31   Max.   :309.00   Max.   :360.00   Max.   :97.0  
##  Sea_Level_Pressure
##  Min.   : 9668     
##  1st Qu.:10086     
##  Median :10161     
##  Mean   :10154     
##  3rd Qu.:10227     
##  Max.   :10447

The raw dataset contains the following features that were recorded daily from 1979 to 2023:

  • DATE: Date in YYYYMMDD format.
  • TX: Daily maximum temperature in 0.1°C.
  • TN: Daily minimum temperature in 0.1°C.
  • TG: Daily mean temperature in 0.1°C.
  • SS: Daily sunshine duration in 0.1 hours.
  • SD: Daily snow depth in 1 cm.
  • RR: Daily precipitation amount in 0.1 mm.
  • QQ: Daily global radiation in W/m².
  • PP: Daily sea level pressure in 0.1 hPa.
  • HU: Daily relative humidity in %.
  • CC: Daily cloud cover in oktas.

For the purpose of my analysis, I decided to focus on the mean temperature (TG) and look at some other features such as precipitation (RR), humidity (HU), and sea level pressure (PP) as well as reduce the period to the most recent two years (2022 to 2023).

# ACFs and Spectral Density
plotts.sample.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp)

## [277] -13.9781067 -12.6513333 -12.7145077 -13.9627346 -15.3044033 -15.2507796
## [283] -14.9821045 -15.2654050 -15.7192123 -16.7083065 -17.4191413 -17.0200911
## [289] -16.5817115 -16.0282919 -15.6463428 -16.1535168 -16.8307772 -16.0398827
## [295] -15.2398437 -15.9592514 -17.9485782 -18.8866855 -17.7275920 -17.2232793
## [301] -18.1770151 -19.6720302 -18.3763158 -15.5871819 -14.4769942 -14.8476092
## [307] -15.2742228 -15.7247059 -17.0265189 -17.3591736 -16.3277487 -16.4659025
## [313] -17.9973809 -19.4189232 -18.8511597 -17.0329917 -16.2471955 -17.2032903
## [319] -18.4177472 -18.0722984 -17.1542516 -16.0831006 -15.7530310 -16.3587852
## [325] -16.4500585 -15.7692973 -15.1537730 -14.7237137 -14.2520704 -13.8795454
## [331] -13.6686167 -13.0443183 -12.9884419 -14.2888373 -15.1736524 -14.7095604
## [337] -14.0067723 -13.5831395 -14.5474573 -17.1637552 -19.3152326 -19.1286840
## [343] -19.2030655 -19.5735933 -18.0154360 -16.3475096 -15.4432615 -14.3080296
## [349] -13.5460326 -14.1732755 -15.9600700 -17.0059938 -15.2048138 -13.0533328
## [355] -12.5561071 -13.5713014 -14.7335478 -15.2805602 -15.8227461 -15.9114702
## [361] -15.7850224 -16.7922056 -18.5370408 -18.8925868 -18.5914806
acf(clean_data$Avg_Temp, lag.max = 364)

plotts.wge(daily_data$TG)

plotts.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp)

Data seems highly nonstationary as there seems to be a clear trend within the realizations. Furthermore, the autocorrelations seem highly autocorrelated at each subsequent lag and damp slowly.

We will suplement this further with a Dickey-Fuller test.

Univariate Models

ARIMA and ARMA models

# Dickey-Fuller test for a unit root: p > 0.05
adf.test(clean_data$Avg_Temp)
## 
##  Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
## 
## data:  clean_data$Avg_Temp
## Dickey-Fuller = -2.6025, Lag order = 8, p-value = 0.3233
## alternative hypothesis: stationary
# differenced once
d1 = artrans.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi.tr = 1, lag.max = 400)

# differenced once and for s = 365
d1.s365 = artrans.wge(d1, phi.tr = c(rep(0,364),1), lag.max = 400)

# s = 365
s365 = artrans.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi.tr = c(rep(0,364),1), lag.max = 400)

# best p and q estimates by AIC and AICC for d1, d1.s365, and s365 respectively
aic5.wge(d1, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aic")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aic 
##     p    q        aic
##     1    3   5.859020
##     1    2   5.860856
##     2    2   5.864978
##     2    1   5.865597
##     3    1   5.867478
aic5.wge(d1, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aicc")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aicc 
##     p    q        aicc
##     1    3    6.861923
##     1    2    6.863713
##     2    2    6.867881
##     2    1    6.868454
##     3    1    6.870381
aic5.wge(d1.s365, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aic")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aic 
##     p    q        aic
##     8    2   6.462857
##     8    3   6.470504
##     9    3   6.473171
##     3    3   6.482202
##     0    3   6.483397
aic5.wge(d1.s365, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aicc")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aicc 
##     p    q        aicc
##     8    2    7.470793
##     8    3    7.478856
##     9    3    7.481971
##     3    3    7.488811
##     0    3    7.489352
aic5.wge(s365, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aic")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aic 
##     p    q        aic
##     1    0   6.436966
##     2    0   6.437269
##     1    1   6.437316
##     6    0   6.437325
##     1    2   6.442539
aic5.wge(s365, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aicc")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aicc 
##     p    q        aicc
##     1    0    7.442628
##     2    0    7.443053
##     1    1    7.443100
##     6    0    7.443912
##     1    2    7.448476
aic5.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aic")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aic 
##     p    q        aic
##     2    2   5.863544
##     3    1   5.864364
##     3    2   5.866317
##     4    1   5.866462
##     6    0   5.866656
aic5.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, p = 0:10, q=0:3, type="aicc")
## ---------WORKING... PLEASE WAIT... 
## 
## 
## Five Smallest Values of  aicc 
##     p    q        aicc
##     2    2    6.866443
##     3    1    6.867263
##     3    2    6.869269
##     4    1    6.869415
##     6    0    6.869669

The Dickey-Fuller test supports our previous claim as we are unable to reject the null hypothesis (p > 0.05). Thus, we will transform our data by differencing d = 1.

I also checked for a few other transformations, but results we futile as the generation of data using those models would be highly unstable.

est.d1 = est.arma.wge(d1, p=1, q=2)
##   
##   
## Coefficients of AR polynomial:  
## 0.6177 
## 
##                            AR Factor Table 
## Factor                 Roots                Abs Recip    System Freq 
## 1-0.6177B              1.6190               0.6177       0.0000
##   
##   
##   
##   
## Coefficients of MA polynomial:  
## 0.7367 0.1153 
## 
##                               MA FACTOR TABLE 
## Factor                 Roots                Abs Recip    System Freq 
## 1-0.8694B              1.1503               0.8694       0.0000
## 1+0.1327B             -7.5371               0.1327       0.5000
##   
## 
mean(d1)
## [1] -0.07956104
est.d1$avar
## [1] 347.1934
spectral_dens = parzen.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, plot = FALSE)
plot(spectral_dens$freq, spectral_dens$pzgram, type = "l", col = "black", lwd = 3, main = "Average Daily Temperature: ARIMA(1,1,2)", xlab = "Frequency", ylab = "Spectral Density (dB)")
for (i in 1:10) {
  generated_series = gen.arima.wge(n=730, phi = est.d1$phi, theta = est.d1$theta, d = 1, s = 0 , mu = mean(d1), vara = est.d1$avar, plot = FALSE, sn = i)
  spec_dens_gen = parzen.wge(generated_series, plot = FALSE)
  lines(spec_dens_gen$freq, spec_dens_gen$pzgram, type = "l", col = "blue", lwd = 1)
}

\(\phi(B)(1-B)(X_t+0.07956104)=\theta(B)a_t\), \(\hat{\sigma_a^2}=347.1934\)
Where \(\phi(B) = 1 - 0.6177B\) and \(\theta(B) = 1 - 0.7367B - 0.1153B^2\)

Our ARIMA(1,1,2) appears alright at first glance. The spectral densities are following the general shape of the original data, however, a little loosely.

est.stationary = est.arma.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, p=2, q=2)
##   
##   
## Coefficients of AR polynomial:  
## 1.5687 -0.5738 
## 
##                            AR Factor Table 
## Factor                 Roots                Abs Recip    System Freq 
## 1-0.9880B              1.0121               0.9880       0.0000
## 1-0.5807B              1.7220               0.5807       0.0000
##   
##   
##   
##   
## Coefficients of MA polynomial:  
## 0.6939 0.1216 
## 
##                               MA FACTOR TABLE 
## Factor                 Roots                Abs Recip    System Freq 
## 1-0.8389B              1.1921               0.8389       0.0000
## 1+0.1450B             -6.8987               0.1450       0.5000
##   
## 
mean(clean_data$Avg_Temp)
## [1] 127.9781
est.stationary$avar
## [1] 347.1808
spectral_dens = parzen.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, plot = FALSE)
plot(spectral_dens$freq, spectral_dens$pzgram, type = "l", col = "black", lwd = 3, main = "Average Daily Temperature: ARMA(2,2)", xlab = "Frequency", ylab = "Spectral Density (dB)")
for (i in 1:10) {
  generated_series = gen.arma.wge(n=730, phi = est.stationary$phi, theta = est.stationary$theta, mu = mean(clean_data$Avg_Temp), vara = est.stationary$avar, plot = FALSE, sn = i)
  spec_dens_gen = parzen.wge(generated_series, plot = FALSE)
  lines(spec_dens_gen$freq, spec_dens_gen$pzgram, type = "l", col = "green", lwd = 1)
}

\(\phi(B)(X_t-127.9781)=\theta(B)a_t\), \(\hat{\sigma_a^2}=347.1808\)
Where \(\phi(B) = 1 - 1.5687B + 0.5738B^2\) and \(\theta(B) = 1 - 0.6939B - 0.1216B^2\)

Even though the data is most likely not stationary (due to our previous analysis), I thought it would be interesting to still model this data using a stationary model using ARMA. Surprisingly, the model seems to follow the spectral density somewhat better.

# short h = 7
# ARMA
fore.arma.short = fore.arma.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi = est.stationary$phi, theta = est.stationary$theta, n.ahead = 7, lastn = TRUE)

mean((fore.arma.short$f - clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730])^2)
## [1] 424.4879
#ARIMA
fore.arima.short = fore.arima.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi = est.d1$phi, theta = est.d1$theta, d = 1, n.ahead = 7, lastn = TRUE)

mean((fore.arima.short$f - clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730])^2)
## [1] 295.0862
rw.arma.short = roll.win.rmse.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, horizon = 7, phi = est.stationary$phi, theta = est.stationary$theta)
## [1] "Please Hold For a Moment, TSWGE is processing the Rolling Window RMSE with 721 windows."

## [1] "The Summary Statistics for the Rolling Window RMSE Are:"
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   4.556  17.167  23.287  26.650  32.927  88.439 
## [1] "The Rolling Window RMSE is:  26.65"
rw.arima.short = roll.win.rmse.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, horizon = 7, d = 1, phi = est.d1$phi, theta = est.d1$theta)
## [1] "Please Hold For a Moment, TSWGE is processing the Rolling Window RMSE with 719 windows."

## [1] "The Summary Statistics for the Rolling Window RMSE Are:"
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   6.628  20.224  28.765  33.739  44.573 113.283 
## [1] "The Rolling Window RMSE is:  33.739"
# long h = 30
# ARMA
fore.arma.long = fore.arma.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi = est.stationary$phi, theta = est.stationary$theta, n.ahead = 30, lastn = TRUE)

mean((fore.arma.long$f - clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730])^2)
## [1] 1263.505
# ARIMA
fore.arima.long = fore.arima.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi = est.d1$phi, theta = est.d1$theta, d = 1, n.ahead = 30, lastn = TRUE)

mean((fore.arima.long$f - clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730])^2)
## [1] 2377.12
rw.arma.long = roll.win.rmse.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, horizon = 30, phi = est.stationary$phi, theta = est.stationary$theta)
## [1] "Please Hold For a Moment, TSWGE is processing the Rolling Window RMSE with 698 windows."

## [1] "The Summary Statistics for the Rolling Window RMSE Are:"
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   12.16   24.97   32.84   36.03   44.99   80.17 
## [1] "The Rolling Window RMSE is:  36.029"
rw.arima.long = roll.win.rmse.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, horizon = 30, d = 1, phi = est.d1$phi, theta = est.d1$theta)
## [1] "Please Hold For a Moment, TSWGE is processing the Rolling Window RMSE with 696 windows."

## [1] "The Summary Statistics for the Rolling Window RMSE Are:"
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   13.98   30.51   40.84   45.74   57.25  133.10 
## [1] "The Rolling Window RMSE is:  45.744"
ylim_l = min(c(fore.arima.long$ll,fore.arma.long$ll,fore.arima.short$ll,fore.arma.short$ll))

ylim_u = max(c(fore.arima.long$ul,fore.arma.long$ul,fore.arima.short$ul,fore.arma.short$ul))

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with ARMA(2,2) Forecasts h = 7")
lines(seq(1,7),fore.arma.short$f,col="green")
lines(seq(1,7),fore.arma.short$ul,col="lightgreen")
lines(seq(1,7),fore.arma.short$ll,col="lightgreen")

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with ARIMA(1,1,2) Forecasts h = 7")
lines(seq(1,7),fore.arima.short$f,col="blue")
lines(seq(1,7),fore.arima.short$ul,col="lightblue")
lines(seq(1,7),fore.arima.short$ll,col="lightblue")

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with ARMA(2,2) Forecasts h = 30")
lines(seq(1,30),fore.arma.long$f,col="green")
lines(seq(1,30),fore.arma.long$ul,col="lightgreen")
lines(seq(1,30),fore.arma.long$ll,col="lightgreen")

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with ARIMA(1,1,2) Forecasts h = 30")
lines(seq(1,30),fore.arima.long$f,col="blue")
lines(seq(1,30),fore.arima.long$ul,col="lightblue")
lines(seq(1,30),fore.arima.long$ll,col="lightblue")

ARIMA(1,1,2) seems to perform better in the short-run (h=7) whereas ARMA(2,2) performs better in the long-run (h=30)

Multivariate models

VAR

ccf(clean_data$Avg_Temp,clean_data$Precipitation)

ccf(clean_data$Avg_Temp,clean_data$Humidity)

ccf(clean_data$Avg_Temp,clean_data$Sea_Level_Pressure)

vardata = clean_data %>% dplyr::select(Avg_Temp, Humidity)

Humidity appears to be the most correlated with Avg_Temperature. Thus, we will use it for multivariate model building.

vardata_train7 = vardata[1:723,]
vardata_train30 = vardata[1:700,]
VARselect(vardata, lag.max = 10, type="const", season=NULL,exogen=NULL)
## $selection
## AIC(n)  HQ(n)  SC(n) FPE(n) 
##      6      6      1      6 
## 
## $criteria
##                   1            2            3            4            5
## AIC(n)     9.899284     9.874464     9.852485     9.841488     9.834987
## HQ(n)      9.914016     9.899018     9.886860     9.885684     9.889005
## SC(n)      9.937445     9.938065     9.941526     9.955969     9.974909
## FPE(n) 19916.108781 19427.883287 19005.547584 18797.707298 18675.956097
##                   6            7            8            9           10
## AIC(n)     9.815180     9.817643     9.824170     9.829432     9.833976
## HQ(n)      9.879019     9.891303     9.907651     9.922735     9.937101
## SC(n)      9.980542    10.008445    10.040412    10.071115    10.101099
## FPE(n) 18309.733360 18354.965042 18475.253000 18572.864123 18657.613921
varmod7 = VAR(vardata_train7, p = 6, type = "both")
preds7=predict(varmod7,n.ahead=7)
mean((clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730]-preds7$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:7,1])^2)
## [1] 655.3363
plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with VAR Forecasts h = 7")
lines(seq(1,7),preds7$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:7,1],col="red")
lines(seq(1,7),preds7$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:7,2],col="salmon")
lines(seq(1,7),preds7$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:7,3],col="salmon")

varmod30 = VAR(vardata_train30, p = 6, type = "both")
preds30=predict(varmod30,n.ahead=30)
mean((clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730]-preds30$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:30,1])^2)
## [1] 1975.817
plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with VAR Forecasts h = 30")
lines(seq(1,30),preds30$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:30,1],col="red")
lines(seq(1,30),preds30$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:30,2],col="salmon")
lines(seq(1,30),preds30$fcst$Avg_Temp[1:30,3],col="salmon")

We have evidence against this data being stationary, thus the poor performance from VAR is expected as VAR will generalize AR (stationary) models.

Neural Network (MLP)

set.seed(1)
Avg_Temp.ts = ts(clean_data$Avg_Temp)
Humidity.ts = ts(clean_data$Humidity)

H_lag14 = stats::lag(Humidity.ts, -14)

valid = complete.cases(Humidity.ts, H_lag14)

Avg_Temp.ts = Avg_Temp.ts[valid]

n = length(Avg_Temp.ts)

train_end_7 = n - 7
train_end_30 = n - 30

Avg_Temp.tsT7 = ts(Avg_Temp.ts[1:train_end_7])
Avg_Temp.tsT30 = ts(Avg_Temp.ts[1:train_end_30])

H_lag14T7 = ts(H_lag14[1:train_end_7])
H_lag14T30 = ts(H_lag14[1:train_end_30])

fit.mlp7 = mlp(Avg_Temp.tsT7, xreg = data.frame(Humidity = H_lag14T7),difforder=1, allow.det.season = FALSE, hd.auto.type = "manual", hd = 5, reps = 10)

fit.mlp30 = mlp(Avg_Temp.tsT30, xreg = data.frame(Humidity = H_lag14T30),difforder=1, allow.det.season = FALSE, hd.auto.type = "manual", hd = 5, reps = 10)
fore.mlp7 = forecast(fit.mlp7, xreg = data.frame(Humidity = H_lag14), h=7)
fore.mlp30 = forecast(fit.mlp30, xreg = data.frame(Humidity = H_lag14), h=30)
mean((fore.mlp7$mean - Avg_Temp.ts[(train_end_7 + 1):n])^2)
## [1] 467.0106
mean((fore.mlp30$mean - Avg_Temp.ts[(train_end_30 + 1):n])^2)
## [1] 8871.478
plot(fit.mlp7)
plot(fit.mlp30)

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with MLP Forecasts h = 7")
lines(seq(1,7),fore.mlp7$mean,col="purple")

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with MLP Forecasts h = 30")
lines(seq(1,30),fore.mlp30$mean,col="purple")

6 inputs, 5 hidden nodes, 10 repetitions.

fit2.mlp7 = mlp(Avg_Temp.tsT7, hd.auto.type = "manual", hd = 5, reps = 10)

fit2.mlp30 = mlp(Avg_Temp.tsT30, hd.auto.type = "manual", hd = 5, reps = 10)
fore2.mlp7 = forecast(fit2.mlp7, h=7)
fore2.mlp30 = forecast(fit2.mlp30, h=30)
mean((fore2.mlp7$mean - Avg_Temp.ts[(train_end_7 + 1):n])^2)
## [1] 702.4649
mean((fore2.mlp30$mean - Avg_Temp.ts[(train_end_30 + 1):n])^2)
## [1] 1280.982
plot(fit2.mlp7)
plot(fit2.mlp30)

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with MLP Forecasts h = 7")
lines(seq(1,7),fore2.mlp7$mean,col="pink")

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with MLP Forecasts h = 30")
lines(seq(1,30),fore2.mlp30$mean,col="pink")

2 inputs, 5 hidden nodes, 10 repetitions. Performs slightly better than our multivariate MLP.

Ensemble Model

Combine ARIMA and MLP Multivariate for short-term and ARMA and MLP Univariate for long-term based off of their ASE values.

# ARIMA + MLP Multivariate
ensemble7 = (fore.arima.short$f + fore.mlp7$mean)/2
ensemble30 = (fore.arma.long$f + fore2.mlp30$mean)/2

# ARMA + MLP Univariate
mean((ensemble7 - clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730])^2)
## [1] 372.892
mean((ensemble30 - clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730])^2)
## [1] 1270.452
plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with Ensemble Forecasts h = 7")
lines(seq(1,7),ensemble7,col="cyan")

plot(clean_data$Avg_Temp[701:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Last n", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), main = "Average Daily Temperature with Ensemble Forecasts h = 30")
lines(seq(1,30),ensemble30,col="cyan")

Final recommendation and forecasts with the model

ARIMA(1,1,2) as our data appears to be non-stationary.

The short-term performance is great, and while the long-term isn’t the best, it does contain the tested values within the confidence intervals. Best to use it solely for short-term forecasting, which will be usually enough for a heads up regarding energy consumption or public utilities maintenance. Long-term forecasts should be done with caution as the forecasting is not very strong - as compared to the short-term.

fore.final.short = fore.arima.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi = est.d1$phi, theta = est.d1$theta, d = 1, n.ahead = 7, lastn = FALSE, plot = FALSE)

fore.final.long = fore.arima.wge(clean_data$Avg_Temp, phi = est.d1$phi, theta = est.d1$theta, d = 1, n.ahead = 30, lastn = FALSE, plot = FALSE)

plot(seq(724,730),clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Day", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), xlim=c(724,738), main = "Average Daily Temperature with ARIMA(1,1,2) Forecasts h = 7")
lines(seq(731,737),fore.final.short$f,col="blue")
lines(seq(731,737),fore.final.short$ul,col="lightblue")
lines(seq(731,737),fore.final.short$ll,col="lightblue")

plot(seq(724,730),clean_data$Avg_Temp[724:730],type="l",col="black",xlab="Day", ylab="Average Daily Temperature",ylim=c(ylim_l,ylim_u), xlim=c(724,761), main = "Average Daily Temperature with ARIMA(1,1,2) Forecasts h = 30")
lines(seq(731,760),fore.final.long$f,col="blue")
lines(seq(731,760),fore.final.long$ul,col="lightblue")
lines(seq(731,760),fore.final.long$ll,col="lightblue")
